citations.narrative:
Four adult dogs were found without physical identification. The dogs were a male Miniature Australian Shepherd named
“Little Red,” an adult female Australian Shepherd named "Coral," an adult female Blue Merle Australian Shepherd named
“Abaloney,” and an adult male Australian Shepherd named “Dinky.” The licensee uses collars and tags as a form of
official identification. These dogs had collars without an official tag on them. The licensee stated she had written their
names, IDs, USDA license number, and the letters USDA on the collars with a permanent marker. However, two collars
were unmarked and the rest had black smudges on the collars with no discernable writing.
An approved method of identification is necessary for APHIS officials to positively identify animals for inspection and to
properly trace their movements. All live dogs over the age of 16 weeks shall be identified by an official tag affixed to the
animal’s neck by means of a collar or shall be identified by a distinctive legible tattoo marking approved by the
Administrator or by a microchip placed under the dog’s skin.
Correct by 3/6/26
This inspection and exit interview were conducted with the facility representative.
n

narrative:
2.50(a)(1) 
   
Time and method of identification. 
Four adult dogs were found without physical identification. The dogs were a male Miniature Australian Shepherd named 
“Little Red,” an adult female Australian Shepherd named "Coral," an adult female Blue Merle Australian Shepherd named 
“Abaloney,” and an adult male Australian Shepherd named “Dinky.” The licensee uses collars and tags as a form of 
official identification. These dogs had collars without an official tag on them. The licensee stated she had written their 
names, IDs, USDA license number, and the letters USDA on the collars with a permanent marker. However, two collars 
were unmarked and the rest had black smudges on the collars with no discernable writing. 
 
An approved method of identification is necessary for APHIS officials to positively identify animals for inspection and to 
properly trace their movements. All live dogs over the age of 16 weeks shall be identified by an official tag affixed to the 
animal’s neck by means of a collar or shall be identified by a distinctive legible tattoo marking approved by the 
Administrator or by a microchip placed under the dog’s skin.  
 
Correct by 3/6/26 
 
This inspection and exit interview were conducted with the facility representative.
